chunk_summary: Metabolic surveillance after severe acute malnutrition and low birth weight focuses on preventing dysglycemia and MASLD through healthy catch-up growth, age-specific monitoring, and dietary strategies to avoid central adiposity and ensure normal development.
learning_objectives: Understand the metabolic risks associated with severe acute malnutrition and low birth weight in children.
Learn age-specific surveillance strategies for monitoring growth and metabolic health.
Identify dietary approaches to promote healthy catch-up growth and prevent central adiposity.
Recognize signs of metabolic dysfunction, such as acanthosis, dysglycemia, and rapid waist gain.
key_facts: Early undernutrition and low birth weight (LBW) are linked to later insulin resistance and metabolic risks, especially in South Asian children.
Rapid catch-up growth can lead to central adiposity, dysglycemia, and MASLD (Metabolic Associated Steatotic Liver Disease).
Under 5 years: Focus on growth recovery and infection prevention; avoid restrictive diets.
At 5–9 years: Monitor waist circumference and blood pressure; reserve blood tests for children with obesity, acanthosis, or family history.
At ≥10 years: Use metabolic testing (lipids, fasting glucose, HbA1c, ALT) for children with catch-up adiposity or metabolic signs.
Dietary strategies should prioritize protein-rich meals (e.g., pulses, eggs, dairy, soy) and avoid sugar-driven calorie loading.
reference_quote: Counsel families that the goal is healthy catch-up with protein and micronutrient protection, not sugar-driven calorie loading. — National Health Mission, India
document_key_topics: Metabolic risks after severe acute malnutrition and LBW
Dietary strategies for healthy catch-up growth
